data.title: Nasdaq-100 crumbles as tech sells off and Powell's remarks fan the flames
data.description:
The Nasdaq-100 index (NDX) tumbled 4.1% on Wednesday, weighed down by a rout in technology stocks and hawkish commentary from Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell.

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) disclosed it had received a notice from the U.S. government requiring a special license to export its H20 graphics processing units and related hardware to China. The world's third-largest company by market cap also warned of a $5.5B hit to its quarterly results from the export controls.

Meanwhile, NVDA rival AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) also cautioned of a $800M hit from export controls to China. 

Additionally, Dutch chip giant ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) missed quarterly bookings expectations and said tariffs were increasing uncertainty for the long term. 

The negative updates from the semiconductor behemoths weighed on the chip space. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) - a key barometer for the industry - slid 6.9%.  

Sentiment took a further beating in the afternoon following remarks from Fed chief Powell. He highlighted a deceleration in U.S. economic growth during the first quarter and reiterated his expectation that the impact of tariffs may prove more severe than initially anticipated.

The sell-off in the Nasdaq-100 (NDX) was broad-based, with 99 out of 100 constituent stocks in the red, underscoring the depth of the market’s retreat. 

Among the hardest hit in the NDX was Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD), which saw its stock plummet by 9.9%, leading the day’s declines.

Below are the 10 worst-performing stocks in the Nasdaq-100 as of 3:15 pm EST. 

No. 10: Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) -7.4%.

No. 9: Tesla (TSLA) -7.5%.

No. 8: On Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:ON) -7.8%.

No. 7: GlobalFoundaries (NASDAQ:GFS) -7.8%.

No. 6: KLA Corporation (NASDAQ:KLAC) -7.9%.

No. 5: Applovin (NASDAQ:APP) -8.1%.

No. 4: ASML Holdings (NASDAQ:ASML) -8.2%.

No. 3: Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) -8.7%.

No. 2: Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) -9.8%.

No. 1: Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) -9.9%.

The broader market weakness also dragged down the Invesco QQQ Trust (NASDAQ:QQQ) —a major ETF tracking the Nasdaq-100. With nearly $300B in assets under management, QQQ fell 4.1% on the day.

Other ETFs with significant exposure to the Nasdaq-100 or the broader Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) also are in the market’s crosshairs: (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NYSEARCA:QLD), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NASDAQ:SQQQ), and (NYSEARCA:QID).

data.title: Nasdaq slumps 4%, Dow sheds 900 points on deepening tech rout, hawkish Powell
data.description:
Wall Street on Wednesday extended its steep losses after Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell expressed concerns about the likely higher-than-anticipated effects from tariffs, including possibly putting pressure on the central bank's dual mandate of stable prices and strong employment. 

Sentiment had earlier taken a beating after some negative updates from the world's biggest semiconductor companies ignited fresh trade war concerns and sent technology stocks spiraling.

Into the final hour of trading, the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) was last -3.05% at 5,231.84 points, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -4.23% at 16,111.83 points. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -2.22% at 39,473.23 points.

Powell, appearing at an event at the Economic Club of Chicago, said in prepared remarks that tariffs were "highly likely to generate at least a temporary rise in inflation." In the subsequent discussion, the central bank chief said policymakers were looking to "make sure" tariffs only caused a one-time increase in prices.    

"Let me translate: the Fed is not expecting to 'look through tariffs.' Quite the opposite is the takeaway here. The text as well as the Q&A read & sound hawkish as a resolute Powell prepares for a disruption to the economy," Joseph Brusuelas, principal and chief economist at RSM US LLP, said on X (formerly Twitter).

Powell was also asked about the turmoil in markets, to which the Fed chair said markets were "struggling with a lot of uncertainty" and "processing historically unique developments." But he also said markets were "orderly" and functioning as expected.   

Looking at the 11 S&P sectors, nine were now in the red. Technology shed -5.4%, topping the losers.

Nvidia (NVDA) slid -10%. The world's third-largest company by market cap on Tuesday said it had been informed by the U.S. government that it would require a special license - indefinitely - to ship its H20 graphic processing units and related hardware to China. 

NVDA rival AMD (AMD) fell -10% as well. It also informed of a new license requirement to ship certain products to China. Both NVDA and AMD expect to take charges related to these export controls. 

Meanwhile, Dutch chip giant ASML (ASML) retreated -8.3% after reporting quarterly bookings that missed estimates. The company also cautioned that tariffs were increasing uncertainty for the long term.

Turning to Wednesday's economic calendar, the spotlight was on the March retail sales report. The headline figure improved +1.4% M/M, while the core figure came in at +0.5%. 

Over in the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields were lower. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last down 5 basis points to 4.29%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 7 basis points to 3.80%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: Citigroup's wealth unit signs partnership with Palantir
data.description:
Banking giant Citigroup (NYSE:C) said in the Q1 earnings presentation that its wealth unit has signed a partnership with the enterprise software company Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR).

The partnership aims to modernize how Citi leverages data and improve client experience and operational agility through enhanced processes such as onboarding, account management, and real-time insights.

data.title: VanEck Gold Miners ETF extends rally amid tariff-driven safe-haven rush
data.description:
VanEck Gold Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:GDX) was on track to close seventh session of gains as its shares rose 2.18% to $51.63 on Wednesday afternoon.

The ETF focused on companies in the materials, metals and mining, as well as gold and silver sectors, has been on an upward trajectory since April 8, following Donald Trump’s tariff announcement. Trump’s move move sparked a wider market turbulence driving investors toward safe-haven assets, boosting demand across the sector.

Between April 8 and April 15, GDX gained nearly 22%. In the year so far, VanEck has soared nearly 55% in stark contrast to more than 9% decline in the wider market.

Even as the US announced a temporary, 90-day pause on the tariffs, Trump’s shifting stance continue to unsettle investors, while a generally weaker dollar also supported gold’s rising demand.

Looking as Seeking Alpha’s quant rating VanEck Gold Miners ETF has been rated a Strong Buy with a score of 4.91 out of 5 supported by an A+ rating for momentum and liquidity. However, Seeking Alpha analysts remained cautious about the stock, rating it Hold.

At the same time, Seeking Alpha analyst Bang For The Buck expects both gold and gold miners to continue outperforming amid a wider market sell-off, which makes GDX an attractive investment, they say.

data.title: Europe’s auto market risks are not a direct consequence of tariff actions – SG
data.description:
The automobile and electric vehicles market in Europe is facing high risks, but they are not a consequence of U.S. President Trump’s tariff increases, according to Société Générale analysts.

Europe’s automobile sector faces mounting threats not from recent tariff adjustments but from Chinese manufacturers’ aggressive expansion, according to analysts.

“The primary risk lies in structural market shifts rather than direct trade policy consequences,” said Michel Martinez, Europe chief economist at Société Générale, noting that Chinese brands shipped one car to Europe every minute in 2024.

While U.S. tariffs on Chinese EVs reached 100% in September 2024, effectively blocking access to that market, “Europe has become the decisive arena for electric vehicle dominance,” Martinez said.

Chinese exports to the U.S. accounted for less than 2% of their total EV shipments last year, redirecting focus to EU markets where imports from China surged 1,591% since 2019.

Chinese automakers captured €12.7B in EU automotive imports last year, representing 17% of all vehicles brought into the bloc.

“This explosive growth reflects strategic targeting of Europe’s mid-range EV segment,” with brands leveraging cost advantages to undercut local manufacturers, Martinez said.

To solidify their position, companies like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) are building European production facilities, including a Hungarian plant set to open in late 2025.

“Local manufacturing helps Chinese firms circumvent tariffs while maintaining price competitiveness,” Société Générale analysts said, with Chinese brands like Chery and Leapmotor pursuing similar strategies.

Mass-market European automakers face disproportionate risk compared to premium brands, though Martinez cautions that “no segment is fully immune.”

Société Générale analysts maintain an underweight stance on the sector, noting that Chinese brands’ potential upmarket moves could pressure luxury manufacturers over time.

The EU’s consideration of lowering U.S. auto tariffs to 2.5% highlights broader trade complexities, as “WTO rules require parity across all trading partners,” Martinez said.

These negotiations occur alongside China’s deepening European foothold, creating dual pressures for policymakers balancing industrial protection and international obligations.

data.title: Crypto firm DWF Labs pumps $25M into Trump-linked WLFI amid U.S. expansion
data.description:
DWF Labs, a crypto market maker and Web3 investment firm, has invested $25M in the decentralized finance protocol and governance platform backed by U.S. President Donald Trump and his sons, it said in a statement dated Wednesday.

The purchase of World Liberty Financial ("WLFI") governance tokens comes as DWF is expanding into the U.S., unveiling a new New York City office, as part of its global growth strategy.

The U.S. expansion positions DWF to bolster relationships with traditional financial institutions exploring blockchain integration, it said, as well as enhance regulatory engagement with policymakers in the country.

“The U.S. is the world’s largest single market for digital asset innovation,” said Andrei Grachev, managing partner of DWF Labs. “Our physical presence reflects our confidence in America’s role as the next growth region for institutional crypto adoption.

President Donald Trump's support for crypto-friendly policies has prompted a number of crypto firms to (re)enter the U.S. market. Earlier, crypto exchange OKX said it's expanding to the U.S. and establishing new regional headquarters in San Jose, California.

DWF's purchase of WLFI tokens secures a governance role in the DeFi project, which features the upcoming dollar-pegged USD1 stablecoin. The company said it will leverage its trading infrastructure to provide liquidity across centralized and decentralized venues.

Last month, WLFI raised $550M through an initial coin offering.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Alcohol retailers want to be in charge of marijuana sales
data.description:
An industry group of American alcohol retailers, in a new policy memo, called for tighter state regulations for cannabis, seeking to restrict the sale of intoxicating marijuana products only to businesses licensed to sell alcohol.

American Beverage Licensees issued the two-page policy document early this month outlining key state-level policy considerations to regulate products containing THC, the main psychoactive compound in cannabis.

The industry group representing bar, tavern, and package liquor store owners argued that beverage alcohol retailers, given their long history of compliance, are best positioned to sell THC products.

“States should restrict the sale of intoxicating THC products to businesses licensed to sell beverage alcohol and that are regularly inspected for compliance with laws aimed at preventing sales to individuals under 21,” ABL said.

Its other policy highlights included a proposal to subject cannabis products to a regulatory framework similar to that of alcohol and licensing requirements for cannabis producers, processors, distributors, and retailers.

data.title: Société Générale: European sectors could face challenges amid rising Chinese imports
data.description:
As the U.S. and China heat up tariff wars, the European Union can “be almost relieved to have been spared the worst of it,” and must find solutions to this undergoing risk, according to Société Générale analysts.

President Donald Trump's decision to pause reciprocal tariffs for 90 days — excluding China — has left Europe facing a 10% tariff, while U.S.-China tariffs now stand at 125% for both sides. Société Générale analysts noted that Europe’s “reluctance to retaliate” stems from WTO adherence and the economic costs of escalation. Also, negotiations are taking priority to avoid further disruptions in the already complex trade landscape.

Macro simulations using the NiGEM model project a 0.5-0.6 percentage point drag on euro area GDP growth this year, and 0.4 points in 2026, analysts said.

Société Générale attributed this to weaker U.S. and Chinese demand, compounded by factors like lower oil prices and Euro (PTEU) appreciation, which could push 2026 growth to 0.5-0.8% and ease the Harmonized Index of Consumer Prices (or HICP) to 1.8%.

European industrial sectors face risks from redirected Chinese exports, particularly in electronics, electrical equipment, and consumer goods like toys and clothing.

“Higher inflows of Chinese goods would lower EU producers’ market power,” though anti-dumping measures might limit price declines and inflation impacts, given their modest weight in the HICP basket, said Michel Martinez, Europe chief economist at Societe Générale, in a note.

Opportunities exist for EU firms to gain market share in U.S. and Chinese markets, especially in toys, sporting goods, and low-value-added household equipment.

According to the note, “European exporters could benefit from detouring/trans-shipping” and improved competitiveness against Chinese rivals in the U.S. and domestic Chinese demand.

The EU’s immediate focus remains securing a deal with the U.S. before the 90-day tariff pause expires, though analysts cautioned that “the best outcome may be maintaining the 10% tariff.”

Société Générale analysts said longer-term solutions might include reviving the EU-China Comprehensive Agreement on Investments or bilateral deals, which could offset risks but requires balancing benefits against dependency concerns.

While lower consumer goods prices might marginally boost EU consumption, analysts stressed that “the overall inflation impact would be limited,” and concluded that Europe’s growth trajectory hinges on managing trade diversion risks while capitalizing on niche export gains, calling it a “delicate equilibrium” of losses and opportunities in the evolving trade environment.

data.title: Looking for tariff-resistant stocks? Think sports technology names
data.description:
Sportradar Group AG (NASDAQ:SRAD) broke to its highest level in more than three years as the sports data company continues to attract investors looking for a company that does not face tariff headwinds.  At the company's investor day event last month, CEO Carsten Koerl and the management team highlighted the company's competitive advantages and its position at a pivotal moment for multi-year value creation. Sportradar (NASDAQ:SRAD) emphasized that its unmatched scale, extensive sports rights, and innovative product portfolio are key drivers for sustainable revenue growth, margin expansion, and cash generation.

Sportradar (SRAD) also released financial targets, including expectations for 2027 revenue of €1.7 billion in revenue, adjusted EBITDA of €455 million, a 700-basis-point margin expansion, and €275 million in free cash flow with at least 60% conversion.

On Wall Street, 12 out of 14 firms covering the sports data stock have a Buy-equivalent rating. Seeking Alpha analysts also lean bullish in general. Redfox Capital Ideas wrote recently that "SRAD's structural moat is strong, the upsell opportunity is clear, and execution on expanding internationally has been solid."

Shares of Sportradar (SRAD) are up 36% on a year-to-date basis, while peer Genius Sports (NYSE:GENI) is 18% higher.

Genius Sports (NYSE:GENI) stands out by having a Seeking Alpha Quant Rating of Strong Buy and one of the highest quant scores in the consumer discretionary sector. Recently, B. Riley Securities started off coverage on GENI with a Buy rating, noting its live sports data is deeply integrated into 650+ sportsbooks with long-term contracts that provide visibility and growth as it adds more leagues and events to the portfolio.
